title: UCB — Immunology — Knowledge Doc
html:
Bimzelx (Bimekizumab) -- UCB Immunology Market Context

1. Disease Landscape

Moderate-to-Severe Plaque Psoriasis (PsO)

Plaque psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disease affecting approximately 7.5 million adults in the United States, with roughly 20-30% classified as moderate-to-severe. The disease manifests as raised, red, scaly plaques most commonly on the scalp, elbows, knees, and trunk, though involvement of high-impact areas (scalp, face, genitals, palms/soles) significantly amplifies quality-of-life burden even at lower body surface area (BSA) involvement. The average moderate-to-severe PsO patient managed by dermatologists in the ZoomRx HCP-Pt study reported managing approximately 97 such patients over a 3-month period, indicating a substantial addressable patient population per prescriber.

Key clinical measures include BSA (body surface area coverage), PASI (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index), IGA (Investigator's Global Assessment), and patient-reported outcomes like NRS (Numeric Rating Scale for itch). Treatment goals have evolved from PASI 75 to PASI 90 and increasingly PASI 100 (complete clearance) as the standard of care, driven by the efficacy of newer biologics.

Scalp psoriasis deserves special attention: ZoomRx conversation data shows scalp involvement was discussed in 17 of 30 conversations (57%), making it the most commonly discussed special site. Persistent itching, flaking, and scaling of the scalp resist topical treatments, and treatments' ability to address scalp symptoms often directly drives prescribing decisions. Seven of those 17 scalp patients also had joint-related issues, creating a dual-burden cohort that is particularly relevant for Bimzelx positioning.

Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA)

Psoriatic arthritis affects approximately 30% of psoriasis patients and represents a significant expansion opportunity. PsA involves inflammatory arthritis with symptoms including joint pain, stiffness, swelling, dactylitis (sausage digits), and enthesitis. ZoomRx data shows joint-related discussions occurred in over half of all PsO conversations collected, with HCPs frequently screening for stiffness, swelling, and dactylitis even when patients did not volunteer joint complaints. Notably, patients often downplay joint symptoms, attributing them to aging or other causes, meaning the true PsA burden in PsO patients may be underestimated.

In the ZoomRx study, of 7 PsO+PsA patients identified, only 1 was co-managed with a rheumatologist; the remaining 6 were managed solely by the dermatologist. This underscores that dermatologists are the primary decision-makers for PsA treatment in patients presenting with skin disease, creating a critical influence point for Bimzelx given its strong dual skin-and-joint positioning.

Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA) and Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS)

Axial spondyloarthritis encompasses both non-radiographic axSpA (nr-axSpA) and ankylosing spondylitis (AS), affecting an estimated 1-1.4% of the population. These conditions are characterized by inflammatory back pain, sacroiliitis, and progressive spinal fusion in AS. The IL-17 class has established efficacy here, with Cosentyx and Taltz as well-established options. Bimzelx received FDA approval for both nr-axSpA and AS in September 2024, entering a competitive but growing market where its dual IL-17A/F inhibition may offer incremental benefit.

Hidradenitis Suppurativa (HS)

Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic, debilitating inflammatory skin condition affecting approximately 1% of the US population, characterized by painful nodules, abscesses, and draining tunnels primarily in intertriginous areas (axillae, groin, inframammary folds). Treatment options have been historically limited, with Humira (adalimumab) being the only approved biologic for HS prior to 2024. The FDA approved Bimzelx for moderate-to-severe HS on November 20, 2024, making it the first IL-17A/F inhibitor approved for this indication. This represents a major growth driver given the large underserved patient population and the high unmet need.

2. Product Profile -- Bimzelx (Bimekizumab-bkzx)

Mechanism of Action

Bimekizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that selectively inhibits both interleukin-17A (IL-17A) and interleukin-17F (IL-17F). This dual inhibition is the key differentiator from other IL-17 inhibitors:

  • Cosentyx (secukinumab): IL-17A only
  • Taltz (ixekizumab): IL-17A only
  • Bimzelx (bimekizumab): IL-17A + IL-17F (dual)

IL-17F contributes independently to inflammatory pathways in psoriasis, PsA, and other immune-mediated conditions. By neutralizing both cytokines, bimekizumab provides more comprehensive pathway inhibition. This dual mechanism is credited with delivering faster onset of action and higher absolute clearance rates in clinical trials.

FDA-Approved Indications (as of April 2026)

  1. Moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (October 2023) -- adults who are candidates for systemic therapy or phototherapy
  2. Active psoriatic arthritis (September 2024)
  3. Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (September 2024) -- with objective signs of inflammation
  4. Ankylosing spondylitis (September 2024)
  5. Moderate-to-severe hidradenitis suppurativa (November 2024)

Five indications approved within 13 months is an unusually rapid cadence and positions Bimzelx as a versatile platform across immune-mediated inflammatory diseases.

Dosing

  • PsO: 320 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, then every 8 weeks. For patients ≥120 kg, consider 320 mg every 4 weeks after week 16.
  • PsA / nr-axSpA / AS: 160 mg SC every 4 weeks
  • HS: 160 mg SC every 4 weeks

The PsO loading dose is more intensive than IL-23 inhibitors (Skyrizi: 150 mg at weeks 0, 4, then every 12 weeks; Tremfya: 100 mg at weeks 0, 4, then every 8 weeks), which creates both a clinical advantage (faster onset) and a practical barrier (more injections in the loading phase).

Key Clinical Data

BE VIVID (vs. ustekinumab, PsO): PASI 90 at week 16: bimekizumab 85% vs. ustekinumab 50%. PASI 100 at week 16: bimekizumab 59% vs. ustekinumab 21%.

BE READY (PsO): PASI 90 at week 16: 91%. PASI 100 at week 16: 68%.

BE RADIANT (vs. secukinumab, PsO): PASI 100 at week 16: bimekizumab 61% vs. secukinumab 49%. At week 48: bimekizumab 67% vs. secukinumab 46%.

BE OPTIMAL / BE COMPLETE (PsA): ACR50 at week 16 significantly superior to placebo. Significant improvements across musculoskeletal and skin endpoints.

BE BOLD (vs. risankizumab, PsA, topline March 2026): Bimekizumab achieved statistically significant superiority over risankizumab on ACR50 at week 16 -- the first head-to-head biologic trial to demonstrate superiority in PsA over an IL-23 inhibitor.

BE HEARD I & II (HS): Treatment response at week 16 was sustained through week 48 in a total enrollment of 1,014 patients.

Safety Profile

Key safety signals that impact real-world prescribing (per ZoomRx HCP-Pt conversation data):

  1. Candidiasis / Oral thrush: The most discussed safety concern in conversations. Approximately 9% incidence in the first 4 months of treatment. HCPs consistently reassure patients that it is treatable (5-7 days of oral antifungal) and typically does not recur. Despite being the most mentioned, it does not significantly deter prescribing.

  2. Depression / Suicidal ideation: Discussed in 6 of 10 Bimzelx conversations. HCPs frame it as rare, unclear if drug-related, and based on a single event in clinical trials. Most HCPs are not personally concerned but find it burdensome to explain given time constraints. The mood screening requirement adds perceived administrative friction.

  3. GI issues / Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD): This is the safety concern with the most direct impact on prescribing decisions. HCPs actively screen for GI/IBD history before prescribing. In the ZoomRx study, one HCP explicitly chose Skyrizi over Bimzelx after the patient reported GI symptoms, stating "IL-17 group can be sensitive for people who already have GI issues." This is a class-wide IL-17 concern, not specific to Bimzelx.

  4. Infection risk: Mentioned as a routine biologic class effect. Immunosuppression manageable with pre-screening and monitoring.

3. Competitive Landscape

IL-17 Inhibitors (Same Class)

Cosentyx (secukinumab) -- Novartis

  • First-to-market IL-17A inhibitor (2015)
  • Approved for PsO, PsA, AS, nr-axSpA, enthesitis-related arthritis
  • Well-established safety and efficacy profile with extensive real-world data
  • Currently outperforms Bimzelx on ease of access and insurance coverage
  • Biosimilar competition approaching (patents expiring), which may reshape economics

Taltz (ixekizumab) -- Eli Lilly

  • IL-17A inhibitor approved for PsO, PsA, AS, nr-axSpA
  • Strong efficacy, particularly valued for joint protection in PsA
  • ZoomRx data shows Taltz is often the IL-17 of choice for HCPs who are "Convert IL-17A Writers" -- comfortable with IL-17 but not yet with Bimzelx specifically
  • In 5 cases where initial injectable failed, IL-17 inhibitors were the next step (Taltz in 2, Bimzelx in 3), suggesting these are reserved for later lines

IL-23 Inhibitors (Different Class, Key Competitors)

Skyrizi (risankizumab) -- AbbVie

  • IL-23 (p19) inhibitor, approved for PsO, PsA, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis
  • Dominant market position: approximately 45% of US biologic prescription share in psoriasis
  • Expected to reach $17.3 billion in global sales in 2025
  • Compelling dosing advantage: every 12 weeks after loading (quarterly maintenance)
  • Excellent skin clearance rates (PASI 90 and 100 data competitive with Bimzelx)
  • Longer drug survival in real-world studies (IL-23 class advantage)
  • Key disadvantage vs. Bimzelx: slower onset of action, weaker joint data (BE BOLD confirmed Bimzelx superiority on ACR50)
  • ZoomRx data: Skyrizi was the most frequently prescribed product in conversations; patients sometimes specifically requested it

Tremfya (guselkumab) -- Johnson & Johnson

  • IL-23 (p19) inhibitor, approved for PsO, PsA
  • Achieved $5.2 billion in 2025 sales (40% growth from 2024)
  • Every 8-week dosing after loading
  • Valued for its safety profile, often positioned when fewer injections are a priority
  • Recently expanded indications
  • In ZoomRx data, one HCP discussed Tremfya for its "less injections" and "good safety profile"

TYK2 / Oral Options

Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) -- Bristol Myers Squibb

  • Oral TYK2 inhibitor, approved for moderate-to-severe PsO
  • Positioned as the "best oral" option for patients with needle aversion
  • ZoomRx data: HCPs acknowledge Sotyktu's efficacy is lower than injectables ("at week 16, about 60% of patients saw some clearance") but offer it when patients refuse injections
  • Captures patients through convenience, not efficacy superiority

Otezla (apremilast) -- Amgen

  • Oral PDE4 inhibitor for PsO and PsA
  • Lower efficacy than biologics but no injection requirement
  • Often a stepping stone before injectable therapy

JAK Inhibitors

Rinvoq (upadacitinib) -- AbbVie

  • JAK inhibitor approved for PsA, AS, among other indications
  • Oral dosing is attractive, but JAK class carries cardiovascular and malignancy safety concerns
  • Not approved for PsO

Market Dynamics Summary

The psoriasis biologics market is a $74+ billion projected opportunity by 2033. The competitive landscape is characterized by:

  1. IL-23 momentum: Skyrizi is the dominant force, capturing share from older TNF inhibitors and IL-17s. The IL-23 class benefits from less frequent dosing, perceived safety advantages, and strong durability data.

  2. Bimzelx counter-narrative: Bimzelx challenges IL-23 dominance with faster onset, higher absolute clearance rates, and now head-to-head PsA superiority data (BE BOLD). The dual IL-17A/F mechanism provides a distinct story.

  3. Access remains a barrier: Both Cosentyx and Taltz currently outperform Bimzelx on ease of market access. Insurance/prior authorization challenges are a cited barrier for Bimzelx.

  4. Familiarity bias is real: ZoomRx data consistently shows HCPs default to what they know. "I haven't used this one before, and I'm not very familiar with the medication, so I'm not very likely to prescribe it" was a verbatim from a Convert IL-17A Writer. Overcoming this inertia is the core commercial challenge.

4. Pipeline Threats and Opportunities

Near-Term Pipeline Threats (2026-2028)

  • Biosimilar secukinumab: Multiple biosimilar versions of Cosentyx are in development, which could reshape IL-17 class economics and increase overall IL-17 prescribing volume
  • IL-23 label expansions: Skyrizi's Crohn's and UC approvals have expanded AbbVie's dermatologist touchpoints, creating spillover prescribing in psoriasis
  • Oral pipeline: Next-generation oral options (TYK2 inhibitors, oral IL-17s) could erode injectable market share, though efficacy gaps persist
  • Tremfya in UC: J&J's Tremfya expansion into ulcerative colitis broadens its competitive reach

Bimzelx Expansion Opportunities

  • HS commercial ramp: The November 2024 HS approval represents the highest-growth potential given limited competition (only Humira, which faces biosimilar erosion)
  • BE BOLD data dissemination: The March 2026 head-to-head superiority over Skyrizi in PsA is a differentiation asset that needs to be translated into prescribing behavior
  • Pediatric psoriasis: Potential label expansion that could capture early-line patients
  • Additional autoimmune indications: UCB exploring further inflammatory conditions

5. Commercial Context

UCB Financial Performance

UCB's 2025 full-year results showed a transformation driven by Bimzelx:

  • Revenue: EUR 7.74 billion (+26% YoY)
  • Bimzelx net sales: EUR 2.23 billion (+200% YoY, up from EUR 607 million)
  • Bimzelx is now UCB's largest product by revenue, already halfway to the company's target of EUR 4+ billion peak sales
  • 2026 guidance: Revenue growth in high single-digit to low double-digit percentage range at constant exchange rates

UCB Sales Force and Promotional Activity

ZoomRx data reveals that of 12 HCPs who reported Bimzelx rep interactions in the past month:

  • Only 4 considered and prescribed Bimzelx
  • The remaining 8 opted for other drugs citing safety concerns, needle aversion, and access challenges
  • Key rep messages focus on: efficacy (PASI scores), quick onset, high ACR scores, PsO and PsA indications, safety, coverage/prior authorization, and patient assistance programs
  • 11 of 12 UCB interactions were with sales reps; only 1 via manufacturer email

This conversion rate (33% from rep interaction to Rx) suggests room for improvement in commercial messaging effectiveness, particularly around overcoming safety perception barriers and access friction.

HCP Segmentation

UCB segments target HCPs into three behavioral categories:

  1. Bimzelx Believers: Most familiar with and confident in Bimzelx, yet even among this group, only 2 of 7 prescribed Bimzelx in the ZoomRx study -- others cited safety concerns or needle aversion
  2. Bimzelx Dabblers: Have some experience but not yet committed. 5 of 9 considered Bimzelx, with 3 prescribing it
  3. Convert IL-17A Writers: Comfortable with IL-17 class (often prescribe Taltz) but lack personal experience with Bimzelx. Most cited lack of familiarity as the key barrier

6. Key Themes for ZoomRx Engagement

What ZoomRx Research Has Revealed

  1. Efficacy drives Rx but safety drives conversation: Efficacy is cited as the #1 prescribing reason but gets minimal conversation time (~10%). Safety occupies ~30% of Bimzelx discussion time. This disconnect is a key insight for UCB commercial strategy.

  2. Familiarity bias is the primary commercial obstacle: HCPs default to products they know. Bimzelx's newness is both a driver (novel, better data) and a barrier (unfamiliar, harder access).

  3. GI screening is the decisive safety checkpoint: Unlike suicidal ideation (which HCPs discuss but generally dismiss as clinically non-concerning), GI history directly changes prescribing. Patients with any GI concerns are steered to IL-23 inhibitors.

  4. Patients want normalcy, not data: Patient decision-making is driven by desire for control and normalcy. They respond to simple language ("best," "great," "fast") not PASI percentages. Behavioral science principles (catastrophic thinking, social proof, cognitive overload) shape patient reception.

  5. Route of administration is patient-driven; product is HCP-driven: In 23 of 30 conversations, HCPs presented a single product within the chosen route. The commercial battleground is influencing which single product the HCP defaults to.

3. Competitive Landscape
IL-17 Inhibitors (Same Class)
Cosentyx (secukinumab) -- Novartis

First-to-market IL-17A inhibitor (2015)
Approved for PsO, PsA, AS, nr-axSpA, enthesitis-related arthritis
Well-established safety and efficacy profile with extensive real-world data
Currently outperforms Bimzelx on ease of access and insurance coverage
Biosimilar competition approaching (patents expiring), which may reshape economics

Taltz (ixekizumab) -- Eli Lilly

IL-17A inhibitor approved for PsO, PsA, AS, nr-axSpA
Strong efficacy, particularly valued for joint protection in PsA
ZoomRx data shows Taltz is often the IL-17 of choice for HCPs who are "Convert IL-17A Writers" -- comfortable with IL-17 but not yet with Bimzelx specifically

Skyrizi (risankizumab) -- AbbVie

IL-23 (p19) inhibitor, approved for PsO, PsA, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis
Dominant market position: approximately 45% of US biologic prescription share in psoriasis

Tremfya (guselkumab) -- Johnson & Johnson

Not approved for PsO

Bimzelx Expansion Opportunities

Revenue: EUR 7.74 billion (+26% YoY)
Bimzelx net sales: EUR 2.23 billion (+200% YoY, up from EUR 607 million)
